6: A direct revelation: 'Love is strong as death, jealousy is fierce as the grave; its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of the LORD.' The Song declares love's unconquerable strength in the face of the two most formidable opponents — death and sheol. The NT's claim is that this declaration is literally, not merely poetically, true in Christ: his love has conquered death (1 Corinthians 15:54-57: Death is swallowed up in victory; thanks be to God who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ) and the love of God in Christ is literally unconquerable (Romans 8:38-39: neither death nor life ... shall be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord). What the Song celebrates as love's aspiration, the resurrection announces as love's accomplished fact.
1: A direct revelation: ‘O that you were like a brother to me.’ The Incarnation is the answer to the exact longing of this verse: Christ became the church’s ‘brother’ in the most complete sense, so that she could embrace him publicly without shame. Hebrews 2:11-12 is the fulfillment: ‘he is not ashamed to call them brothers, saying, “I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise.”’ The social shame that the beloved feared (being looked down upon for public embrace) is precisely what Christ absorbs in the crucifixion: ‘despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows’ (Isaiah 53:3). The shame that social convention threatened, Christ took; the brotherhood the beloved longed for, Christ became.
2: A type: ‘I would bring you into my mother’s house’ — the deepest hospitality, offering the most intimate domestic space to the beloved. The Lord’s Supper is the church’s bringing of Christ into her house — the table set with the spiced wine and the bread that Christ provided. ‘Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone opens the door, I will come in to him and eat with him, and he with me’ (Revelation 3:20). The beloved’s offering of pomegranate nectar and spiced wine to the one she brings into her house is the church’s eucharistic act — she receives the Bridegroom’s gift and offers her own thanksgiving in return.
3: A type: the left-hand-under-the-head and right-hand embrace — the total protective posture — is the church’s experience of Christ’s holding of her. Deuteronomy 33:27: ‘the eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms.’ John 10:28-29: ‘I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father’s hand.’ The Song’s intimate embrace is the physical image of the theological guarantee of perseverance: the church is held from below (the left hand) and surrounded (the right hand).
4: A type: the adjuration not to stir up love until it pleases is the church’s posture of waiting for the parousia. James 5:7-8: ‘Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. See how the farmer waits for the precious fruit of the earth, being patient about it, until it receives the early and the late rains. You also, be patient. Establish your hearts, for the coming of the Lord is at hand.’ The love that ‘cannot be forced’ is the eschatological love that arrives in its own time — and the church that has experienced its fullness knows not to try to manufacture what only God can give. The patience of the refrain is the patience of the eschatological people.
5: A type: ‘Who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved?’ The Incarnation is God coming ‘up from the wilderness’ of human history, and the church who leans on him is already being carried by his strength. Isaiah 40:31: ‘they who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles.’ The apple tree under which the beloved was born — and under which his mother bore him — is the specific place of new life; Christ was born in the lowliest of places (the manger, like a hidden apple tree in the wilderness) so that the church could find her beginning under his shade. The one who comes up from the wilderness is also the one who was tempted in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11) and came through it to begin his public ministry of love.
7: A direct revelation: ‘Many waters cannot quench love, neither can floods drown it. If a man offered all the wealth of his house for love, it would be utterly scorned.’ The flood-chaos imagery (many waters, rivers) represents the full force of opposition — persecution, death, the powers arrayed against the church. Christ’s love for the church has passed through the flood-chaos of the cross and emerged unquenched: ‘neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord’ (Romans 8:38-39). The contempt for the man who tries to buy love with wealth is the Song’s rejection of the commercial model of salvation — grace cannot be purchased, only received.
8: A type: the brothers’ care for the little sister not yet developed anticipates Christ’s protection of the immature and small in his kingdom. ‘See that you do not despise one of these little ones. For I tell you that in heaven their angels always see the face of my Father who is in heaven’ (Matthew 18:10). The brothers who deliberate about what to do for their sister before she is marriageable represent the care of the community for the young and vulnerable — a care that Christ himself models: he rebukes the disciples for turning away children (Matthew 19:14) and declares that the kingdom belongs to such. The church’s care for her little ones is the echo of the brothers’ protective love.
9: A type: the wall/door discernment — responding to what the beloved is with appropriate care — is the pattern of Christ’s pastoral knowledge of the church. Rev 2-3 (the letters to the seven churches) shows Christ applying exactly this discernment: to Smyrna (strong under persecution) he gives honor; to Laodicea (lukewarm) he offers the correction that secures. Christ’s knowledge of his church is complete and particular: he does not apply the same pastoral response to every situation but responds to what each church actually is. The grace that fortifies the wall and secures the door is the grace of the divine Physician who knows the condition of each patient.
10: A direct revelation: ‘I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers; thus I have become in his eyes as one who finds shalom.’ The church that is a wall — firm in faith, not dissolved by pressure — becomes in Christ’s eyes the bearer of his own peace. Christ is the Prince of Peace (sar shalom, Isaiah 9:6); the church that embodies his character of integrity becomes the instrument of his shalom. Philippians 4:7: ‘the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.’ The guarding-peace of God is the tower that the church becomes in Christ’s eyes when she stands firm. The shalom/shlomoh wordplay is christological: the beloved who finds shalom finds it through her union with the Solomon-Bridegroom who is himself Peace.
11: A type: Solomon’s vineyard at Baal-Hamon, leased to keepers for silver, is the image of the world-vineyard managed by imperfect stewards — the parable of the wicked tenants (Matthew 21:33-41). Christ is the owner of the vineyard (John 15:1: ‘I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser’) who has entrusted the care of his vineyard to the church. The thousand silver pieces due to Solomon anticipate the accountability that the parable foregrounds: the servants who were given the vineyard must account for what it produced. The church is the faithful tenant who returns not just the rent but the fruit itself.
12: A type: ‘My own vineyard is before me; the thousand are for you, O Solomon.’ The church’s free self-gift — ‘my vineyard is mine to give, and I give it to you’ — is the mirror of Christ’s own free self-giving: ‘No one takes [my life] from me, but I lay it down of my own accord’ (John 10:18). The reciprocity is the heart of the covenant: Christ gives himself freely for the church (Ephesians 5:25), and the church responds by freely giving herself to Christ (2 Corinthians 8:5: ‘they gave themselves first to the Lord’). The vineyard that is freely given anticipates the New Jerusalem where all things are freely given: ‘to the thirsty I will give from the spring of the water of life without payment’ (Revelation 21:6).
13: A type: ‘O you who dwell in the gardens, with companions listening for your voice — let me hear it.’ Christ’s desire to hear the church’s voice is the motive for prayer: he invites, even requests, the voice of the one he loves. The companions listening are the cosmic witnesses — the angels who ‘long to look’ into the gospel (1 Peter 1:12) and gather around the worship of the church to hear the voice of the people God has redeemed. The church’s worship is a cosmic event heard by the heavenly assembly: ‘you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to innumerable angels in festal gathering’ (Hebrews 12:22). Every time the church gathers to pray, her voice goes out to a gallery that extends beyond what she can see.
14: A direct revelation: ‘Flee, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.’ The Song’s final line is the church’s cry for the parousia. The beloved running swiftly over the fragrant mountains is the return of Christ that the whole NT ends with: ‘Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!’ (Revelation 22:20). The invitation to flight/swift return — berach dodi (flee, my beloved) — is not dismissal but the urgency of longing: come quickly. The Song that began with ‘let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth’ (Song of Solomon 1:2) ends with the beloved in motion, still coming. The incompleteness of the ending is the ‘not yet’ of the eschatological kingdom: the Bridegroom is on his way, and the Bride waits and calls for his coming.
